@wuub: Yeah, that is true :) But it's still wise to take care. Large files, even though the code in them are simple tend to end up as god classses, knowing way to much in the end thus being complex anyways. Good idea with a separate place to discuss this!

Sadly, we can't have it both ways: avoiding oversplitting on abbreviations and undersplitting on poor or hard to deduce orthography; segtok makes a choice for the former: It uses continuations (segtok/segmenter.py, lines 85ff) to detect words that typically form continuations and do not occur that frequently at the beginning of sentences. "to" is such a case, as are a number of others (lines 85ff).

So the issue you raise here implies that it might be interesting to investigate if it is worth it to shorten this list of continuations or not. In general, I have seen that using these continuations is very helpful, so it  would be a mistake to remove them altogether. However, it would be great to find some empirical evidence if all continuations in use are indeed not frequently found at the beginning of sentences.

I would suggest to investigate something similar to the following snippet on a rather large corpus:
```python
corpus = open("corpus.txt").read()

for cont in CONTINUATIONS:
    cont_cap = r"[" + cont[0] + cont[0].upper() + r"]" + cont[1:]
    val = len(re.findall(r"\.\s+" + cont_cap, corpus)) / \
        len(re.findall(r"[^.]\s+" + cont, corpus))
    print(val, cont)
```
for each continuation in use (lines 85ff). Then, one would order them by this value, and see if there are any that have an above ratio that is significantly larger than most others (kind of "outliers"). Those continuations then indeed could be good candidates for removal, as that means they probably (!) are more frequently used at the beginning of sentences than the others.

A __copy()__ method would be useful. I'll add it. __copy()__ is Python's nomenclature for a shallow copy (dict, set, deque, etc), over __clone()__.

In the mean time, you can create an inline copy with __furl()__ and base_url's URL string

```python
>>> copy = furl(base_url.url).set(path='/method/1')
```

In testing for the above I found a bug - the __furl()__ constructor should accept another furl object, not just a URL string.

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/')
>>> copy = furl(f)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/furl/furl.py", line 659, in __init__
    self.load(url) # Raises ValueError on invalid url.
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/furl/furl.py", line 668, in load
    tokens = urlsplit(url) # Raises ValueError on malformed IPv6 address.
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/furl/furl.py", line 1022, in urlsplit
    toks = urlparse.urlsplit(url)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/urlparse.py", line 173, in urlsplit
    i = url.find(':')
AttributeError: 'furl' object has no attribute 'find'
```

I am consolidating both __copy()__ and the __furl()__ constructor bug in this issue, issue #11.

Yes you are right. Some cast must be done at some point, but I don't think it has to violate strict aliasing. Here is my understanding of the situation:

Strict aliasing means that a type must not be accessible by a pointer of a different type that is not either `void*` or `[signed|unsigned] char*`. In this case you are casting `PFNGLXGETPROCADDRESSPROC_PRIVATE*` to `void**`. `void**` is a different type and not one of the exceptions. Note that a pointer to anything (`void*`) is different to a pointer to a pointer (`void**``). 

Previously there was a cast from `void*` to `void* (*)(const char*)`(`PFNGLXGETPROCADDRESSPROC_PRIVATE`) which seems ok, since `void*` can be a pointer to anything. What it actually points to is outside the scope of C and Glad. You could imagine that somewhere there is actually a function of the type `void* ()(const char*)`. A pointer to it was casted to `void*` somewhere just to return it from `dlsym`. Inside Glad it is than casted back to `void* (*)(const char*)`. Thats all fine because the function was never reachable by a pointer which is not one of the exceptions. This seems similar to casting the result of `malloc` to anything you want for the first time.

Hey @xiaosimao! (little four hair?)

First of all, in your spec, the `system.planets` is just a destination key in the output. Only the value of the dict is used for lookup.

Now, the working code/tutorial:

```python
import glom 
target = {'system': {'planets': [{'name': 'earth'}, {'name': 'jupiter'}, {'name2': 'jupiters'}]}}

glom.glom(target, {'planets': ('system', 'planets', [glom.Coalesce('name', 'name2')])})
# {'planets': ['earth', 'jupiter', 'jupiters']}
```

One step at a time:

1. `{'planets': ... }` -- We set the output to be a dictionary with key `'planets'`, per your request.
1. ` ('system', 'planets', ...)` -- We use a tuple to look up `'system'`, then `'planets'`.
1. `[...]` -- We use a list to traverse the planets
1. `glom.Coalesce('name', 'name2')` -- We use a `Coalesce`, because the planet with `"jupiters"` has its name under key `'name2'` instead of `'name'` like the others.

I hope that makes sense! It's a lot of power for just one line, and I'm sure you'll get the hang of it with some more practice :)

Ok, so I moved the code over to boolexpr (pushed on the `boolexpr` branch), and it doesn't seem to have improved anything.

The formula transformations seem to be slower than those for pyeda, though I didn't test all of the pyeda versions. simplify() takes around ~0.1 seconds,  tseytin() takes ~0.5 seconds. tseytin does properly handle the cases that were simplified to constants. to_cnf() takes ~10 seconds, which is much slower than pyeda was.

I don't really know how to compare the size of the generated output, since len(xs) and size() work differently, but it seems much larger at first glance. size() returns 24974 before simplification, 24856 after simplify, 119840 after tseytin, 119144 after simplify+tseytin, ~16947 after to_cnf, and ~17187 after simplify+to_cnf. to_cnf still has some variability in the size of its output, +/- 100 or so.

Though the transformations themselves are slower, it seems that boolexpr's sat() is actually much faster. The total time of generating the formula and running sat() was about the same with or without tseytin(), making the transformation seem unnecessary, at least in this case.

Sadly, it seems that the instability is actually in the SAT solving process itself, because I saw the same issue as before even without any simplification or cnf conversion. Most of the time the formula is unsatisfiable, but sometimes it finds one that it shouldn't. I just noticed sooner because I was able to run a lot more tests at the higher speed. satisfy_one() on pyeda was taking ~30s I think, so I didn't run very many tests, and just assumed it was to_cnf's fault because of the nondeterministic conversion.

I also saw an interesting exception go by once:
```
Exception ignored in: <bound method Context.__del__ of <boolexpr.wrap.Context object at 0x7ffff2dc1400>>                                                                                             
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/boolexpr/wrap.py", line 295, in __del__
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'boolexpr_Context_del'
Exception ignored in: Exception ignored in: Exception ignored in: Exception ignored in: <bound method Context.__del__ of <boolexpr.wrap.Context object at 0x7ffff1a68d30>>                           
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/boolexpr/wrap.py", line 295, in __del__
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'boolexpr_Context_del'
```

So what are my options for next steps? If it's the SAT solver, I'm not really sure what can be done to fix it.

Apologies for the very late reply.

It seems my problem is quite unique. I have a huge number of features (compared to the dataset size), almost all of them have missing values, the locations of the missing values are not distributed homogeneously, and feature imputation is not straightforward as there are features with very particular meaning that cannot simply be averaged.

Now, some of the features are only present in a small subset of the dataset, but happen to be very informative for that subset. I want my feature selection to figure that out, and then perhaps train an "expert subsystem" specializing on it. In some other cases, I will want to single out features that are worth imputing. As I mentioned, imputation is not straightforward and would need to be done on a one by one basis, but I'd like to know there is value in a feature before any time is spent on filling the missing values.

I had my own pipeline for sequential selection built with these things in mind, and was wondering if I could use your implementation instead. The root of my problem seems to be this limitation with `scikit-learn`: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/4143

I can work my way around it for the time being, when that sklearn issue gets sorted out the solution will be as simple as implementing a `DropNA` transformer.

I'm not sure why this is a bug? If you don't pass `issuer=` to `decode()`, we're assuming that you don't care about the `iss`. The JWT spec does not specify any sort of mandatory validation logic on `iss` if it is present. The only reason we even have the `issuer=` arg is to save the consumer some code if they care to only accept tokens with a specific `iss` (a common case). I would expect a lot of JWT issuers would specify an `iss` but many clients might not care about who the `iss` actually is as long as it passes signature verification.

Maybe I'm missing it but this doesn't look like a bug.

During an email conversation with @cmaumet, she asked whether we support JSON-LD export. I created the branch to share the code I developed earlier this year for this with her.

The JSON-LD export code generates JSON-LD directly without going via a RDF graph. We originally developed it to address the issue of PROV-JSON being not streaming-friendly (due to its highly-nested objects). In our JSON-LD representation, each PROV record is serialised into one JSON object, which can be deserialised on its own (in conjunction with the active JSON-LD context). Hence, each record can be deserialised immediately once its JSON object has been completely read. A normal JSON-LD export does not guarantee this.

Regarding the structure packing on other platforms, I currently can only test on 32 and 64 bit windows, so far I worked with 32 bit windows. Looking further at the C# bindings I see
```C#
// This structure is for backwards binary compatibility on Linux and OSX only
[StructLayout(LayoutKind.Sequential, Pack = 4)] public struct VRControllerState_t_Packed
```
which indicates that (previous) versions on those platforms where forced to align on 4 byte boundaries. AFAIK the default compiler behavior is to align on the size of the largest field. In this case that would be 8 bytes, which was the case for me yesterday because I saw a `sizeof` of 64.

The "normal" struct in C# is defined like this:
```C#
[StructLayout(LayoutKind.Sequential)] public struct VRControllerState_t
```
where it is using the default packing, similar to how `ctypes` would pack structures if no explicit `_pack_` is given. So I think pyopenvr should be good on other platforms, except for the specific case where versions of libs have `VRControllerState_t` that are explicitly packed on 4 bytes. If this is true then pyopenvr would ideally need a similar hack as is done in the C# bindings. I don't have much experience with these kind of things, not can I test any of this. Also if I understand it correctly this would only be a problem if someone is using an older version of the libs, in which case upgrading could be a fix.

> Interesting, how did you come to this conclusion? According to the internet (http://blag.ahax.de/post/421939327/recovering-a-deleted-branch-using-git-reflog) and my own (admittedly quick) test git actually should keep the reflog for deleted branches.

What we're seeing, both in our experiments and on that web page, is that the reflog for HEAD is always around.  If you were to run that same experiment in the blog, but then type 'git reflog gamelan', it would complain that 'gamelan' does not exist, because it was deleted.  

'gamelan' happens to exist in the HEAD reflog because it was recently checked out.  If it had been a year since anyone had checked out the 'gamelan' branch, and then the branch was deleted, then there would likely be no reflog pointing at that branch anymore, and no way to get it back.  So the two reset commands I offered above were specifically geared toward guaranteeing that there is a recent reflog entry in HEAD for the soon-to-be-deleted branch.  

We're only supposed to delete branches which are merged into other branches, but in the unlikely event we do the wrong thing, or that the user panics and realizes they really needed to know what merged commit the deleted branch was pointing to, they'll always be able to recover that information... for a time.

In most cases, you should be able to build your own column type to do exactly what you want, and it is pretty straightforward, even if it is mostly undocumented.

```
class KittenColumn(rom.Column):
    _allowed = KittenPaw
    def _to_redis(self, value):
        # turn the KittenPaw value into a 2.x string or 3.x bytes and return it
    def _from_redis(self, value):
        # value can be one of:
        # None, a KittenPaw object, or a serialized KittenPaw object returned from _to_redis
        # return None or the KittenPaw object
```

If you want to index this data, you would need to write your own keygen function.

Note that rom doesn't do anything special with mutable columns, so if your object is mutable, you will need to explicitly set the column value after changing your attribute. As in:

```
kitten = FluffyKitten.get(id)
kitten.paw._paw = 'hello'
kitten.paw = kitten.paw # necessary for the .save() to know the kitten.paw column changed
kitten.save()
```

I had the same issue. Then found this issue by Google.
I am reading with a Python script an Access *.mdb file that is used by a Windows application and get this UnicodeDecodeError randomly when handling data from cursor.columns.
I printed the .remarks field on my python test script and there was random junk at the end of those fields. It is a matter of luck if and when the junk causes decode errors.

Then I opened the Access DB with Access itself and deleted the description fields, making them empty. After that there were no more problems, the  .remarks field on my python output are empty and no more junk is seen, no more crashes.

@jdelight Apologies for a delayed reply, lost track of this one. You can use the "oxml layer" to do what you want; actually a combination of some base lxml API and some of the oxml wrapper. The oxml layer is just the code in pptx.oxml that provides some wrapper on lxml. The key modules are probably oxml (__init__.py), oxml.ns, and oxml.xmlchemy.

Basically I'd find the element of interest with XPath:

    element = other_element.xpath('.//c:title')[0]

then use oxml/lxml operations to add some stuff after:

    new_subtree = parse_xml(new_xml)
    element.addnext(new_subtree)

The lxml API for the _Element class is here:
http://lxml.de/api/lxml.etree._Element-class.html

All XML elements in python-pptx derive from that class and also from oxml.xmlchemy.BaseOxmlElement.

If you search around on 'python-pptx OR python-docx workaround function' you'll find some other approaches.

I recommend using `-s`/`--strip` option to get smaller function names.   It might even make sense to make it the default at some point, given I suspect C++ template functions will only tend to get larger and larger...

Hi.

"datas" inside a strategy in backtrader are referenced following the order in which they were inserted in the system (a cerebro entity)

"datas" can actually (and mostly do) carry a name which can be a "ticker", a "filename" or any other arbitrary things.

The rationale behind not "referencing" by name is that, imho, a strategy should be as generic as possible and based on sound ideas, rather than being optimized for very specific parameters and a specific "ticker".

Obviously there is no absolute truth and specific parameters and ticker-based strategies can of course be successful.

To indicate on which data you want to operate with the buy/sell/close methods included with the Strategy base class you do the following:

  - self.buy(data=self.data, price=price, ...)

This indicates the action has to be done for the 1st data (also available under the alias self.data0). This is the default and as such you can do:

  - self.buy(price=price, ...)

For data1 and subsequent it should be fairly obvious:

  - self.buy(data=self.data1, price=price, ...)

The following could be potentially added:

  - A function to get a data looking for its "name". Something like: self.getdatabyname(dataname)

  - buy/sell/close could check if the "data" kwarg is a string and if it is, use the self.getdatabyname(dataname) call internally before further proceeding.

The souce code contains several samples and many have gotten a post in www.backtrader.com

For multi-datas the following link shows the multi-datas example:
  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-09-03-multidata-strategy/multidata-strategy/

The sample is in the source code:
  - https://github.com/mementum/backtrader/blob/master/samples/multidata-strategy/multidata-strategy.py

```python
class Feed(models.Model):

    source = models.CharField(max_length=30)
    url = models.URLField()
    file = models.FileField(upload_to='feeds', blank=True)
    last_modified = models.DateTimeField(blank=True, null=True)
    interval = models.IntegerField(
        null=True,
        validators=[MinValueValidator(RQ_SCHEDULER_INTERVAL)]
    )

    def __str__(self):
        if self.file:
            return '{0}:{1}'.format(self.source, str(self.file))
        return super(Feed, self).__str__()

    def fetch(self):
        request = requests.get(self.url, stream=True)
        if request.status_code != requests.codes.ok:
            logger.critical('{0} - Wrong status code: {1}'.format(
                str(self),
                request.status_code
            ))
            return False

        last_modified = email_utils.parsedate_to_datetime(
            request.headers['last-modified'])

        self.refresh_from_db()
        if last_modified == self.last_modified:
            return

        self.last_modified = last_modified

        parsed_url = urlsplit(self.url)
        filename = parsed_url.path.split('/')[-1]

        with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as temp_file:

            query_strings = parse_qs(parsed_url.query)
            if 'gZipCompress' in query_strings \
                    and query_strings['gZipCompress'] == ['yes']:

                compressed_stream = BytesIO(request.content)
                gzipper = gzip.GzipFile(fileobj=compressed_stream)
                temp_file.write(gzipper.read())

            else:
                temp_file.write(request.content)

            temp_file.flush()
            self.file.save(filename, File(temp_file))

        self.save()
        return True

    def parse(self, parser):
        parser(self).parse()
```

I like your idea about creating a partition per organization id. Because a partition is just a table we can use one of the following approaches to restrict access to it on a database level:

1) Create a user/password per organization and only allow them to access their respective table (probably not very convenient if you will have thousands of organizations)
2) PostgreSQL 9.5. will introduce an ability to create a policy for a table which looks like exactly what you're looking for, see [docs](http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/static/sql-createpolicy.html). But unfortunately it's still in alpha if I'm not mistaken.

These are the only things that comes to mind.

You can also think about implementing your security layer in your application instead of a database because I don't think that you will give your organizations a direct access to your database. That would be a much simpler solution.

I have found a bug. You're _already_ reporting L3 cache size for Linux from `/proc/cpuinfo`, but erroneously misrepresenting it as L2. 

       returncode, output = DataSource.cat_proc_cpuinfo()
       ...
       cache_size = _get_field(False, output, None, '', 'cache size')
       info = {
           ...
          'l2_cache_size': cache_size,
       }

From [the man page for `lscpu`](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/lscpu.1.html), which consults `cpuinfo`, the cache value returned there is shared across all processors, meaning it is L3. 

I have verified this by comparing the results of `dmidecode -t cache` with the values contained in `/proc/cpuinfo` - the L3 cache reported there is the same as the cache size value reported there. 

Should I commit a reporting change as part of this change? There are a lot of [alternate ways](https://superuser.com/questions/55776/finding-l2-cache-size-in-linux) to find L2 cache, mind.

It might be a good idea to weight the advantages of threading vs multiprocessing for this task. Most computers nowadays are built with multi-core processors.

A multiprocessor implementation of this example would allow multi_adapter.py to run the processes for several adapters in parallel on separate processors. Of course the adapter would still need to wait for each adapter to finish processing, but running them in parallel would still accomplish this faster even if a few adapters were lagging behind the others.

Also, (I have to verify this) but I believe that threading would be restricted to a single core. I do think threading would be quite useful for tasks in _some_ adapters such as where a bit of background processing could provide an advantage. (For instance, an email IO adapter could use a thread to periodically check for new emails, if there were new ones it would add them to a queue for the next time it was called to retrieve data. This would remove the need to wait for a web request to return with the updated information every time the adapter is used.)

Well, I implemented the whole thing only to find out that ``struct_time`` is an instance of ``tuple`` and therefore cannot have non-standard serialization. Similar to decimal (#21) and numpy scalars (#18), except there's no ``parse_tuples`` flag as far as I can tell...

It's possible to do a solution similar to numpy scalars, having a separate function which replaces ``struct_time`` instances by primitives before encoding. Or they could be encoded as is, and there could be an option (not default) to decode any length-9 list of integers as ``struct_time``.

[Here's a patch](https://gist.github.com/mverleg/087111f0b03d99b7c85f5072f0392acc) that might've worked if tuples could have custom encoders. In case you want to adapt it.

On a side note... ``struct_time`` seems like a bad way to handle time, with no timezones and several redundant and possibly inconsistent fields. It seems useful for communicating with other languages, but the format isn't the same as most other languages which use C convention (week starts on Sunday, months start at 0...). Any reason not to just use datetimes?

You are right. I misled myself before. It is no possible to meet SNI caused hostname issue with `urllib`/`urllib2`.

- Python < 2.7.9 + `urllib`/`urllib2`: no SSL/TLS verifying.
- Python < 2.7.9 + `requests`: SSL/TLS verifying without SNI support. (e.g. `https://tonyseek.com` could not be verified here)

Hello, @jdotjdot !

Can I ask you how you add tags to messages? Do you want to send different tags for different messages of mark them all with a single tag? And how would you send messages - ``send_mail``, ``get_connection`` or ``message.send()``?

There are a few options to work with tags.

1. Use ``get_connection`` to pass ``Tag`` information to ``EmailBackend`` instantiation. And update ``send_messages`` accordingly. Something like this:

```python
class EmailBackend(BaseEmailBackend):

    def __init__(self, token=None, fail_silently=False, tag=None, **kwargs):
        super(EmailBackend, self).__init__(fail_silently=fail_silently)
        self.tag = tag
    ...
    def send_messages(self, email_messages):
        ...
        response = self.client.emails.send_batch(*prepared_messages, TrackOpens=self.get_option('TRACK_OPENS'), Tag=self.tag)
        ...
```
2. Global tag value. The same implementation as ``TRACK_OPENS``
3. Modify email message. You're able to attach tag to email instance as an attribute. Default ``django.core.mail.send_mail`` doesn't allow any extra arguments, thus you can do the following.

```python
from django.core import mail

message = mail.EmailMessage(
    'Subject', 'Body', 'sender@example.com', ['receiver@example.com']
)
message.tag = 'Tag value'
message.send()
```

Django uses ``SafeMIMEText`` internally, thus tag should be attached to the message instance.

postmarker/django.py
```python

    def send_messages(self, email_messages):
        ...
        prepared_messages = [self.prepare_message(message) for message in email_messages]
        ...

    def prepare_message(self, message):
        instance = message.message()
        instance.tag = getattr(message, 'tag', None)
        return instance
```


postmarker/models/email.py
```python

class Email(BaseEmail):

    ...

    @classmethod
    def from_mime(cls, message, manager):
        ...
        tag = getattr(message, 'tag', None)
        return cls(manager=manager, From=sender, To=to, TextBody=text, HtmlBody=html, Subject=subject, Cc=cc, Bcc=bcc,
                   ReplyTo=reply_to, Attachments=attachments, Tag=tag)
```

Do you need any more information from my side?

40000 tests done, and OpenSSH always created 1024 bits size keys! So, creating 1023 bits size key should be an **specific Putty behavior**.

Currently, an OpenSSH server will restrict RSA SSH keys to a minimum of 768 bits (not 767 bits). Try to login with a 767 bit key and you will be denied. I think in a near future (some years) this value will be raised to 1024 (not 1023 bits) or 2048 (not 2047).

Cisco IOS SSH Server implementations also require a minimum of 768 bits (not 767 bits) [link here](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/ios-xml/ios/sec_usr_ssh/configuration/15-s/sec-usr-ssh-15-s-book/sec-secure-shell-v2.html#GUID-6E850547-B842-48FE-98E9-31975293D454). And I suppose if someday Cisco increases its minimum requirements to 1024 bits, it will be 1024 bits (not 1023).

Mikrotik RouterOS SSH Server currently restricts RSA keys to a minimum of 1024 bits. 1023 bits keys are denied by Mikrotik.

So, although I understand the Putty's arguments, I thing `RSA_MIN_LENGTH_*` should keep the current values, not them less 1. Suppose that `sshpubkeys` allowed a 1023 bit key (or 767 bit key, in loose mode) and considered it secure. This key will be denied by SSH servers - that will consider them insecure. This would be an inconsistent behavior. In other words, sshpubkeys should be consistent with OpenSSH Server/Cisco IOS SSH Server/Mikrotik RouterOS SSH Server and other SSH Server implementations (instead of be consistent only with the Putty client).

So I think the pull request should not be merged.

```python
from django.utils.deprecation import MiddlewareMixin

class CustomUserAgentMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin, UserAgentMiddleware):

settings.py

We don not use the same format as Django since Python does not allow `-` in function parameters. We use function parameters to filter out the languages in Django orm as:

```
table.filter(translations__en_us__fieldname='Hello')
```

Otherwise we needed to replace all language codes before the query. That's why I did not bother using them, instead used underscores at the time. That being said it is nice to have such a feature. We can make it optional in the settings to use whatever format you want and replace them before the query if it uses dashes instead of underscores. It has to be optional otherwise it won't be backwards compatible and would break current users.

Hello,

Well, it seems unwise to blindly copy unknown third-party module's properties into another model. It might work, it might break other modules, or it might even corrupt the database.

You can do it on a case-by-case basis, just like you did (I can tell you that `hvad` will not have any problem with what you did here), but systematizing the process would be too dangerous.

Interaction between queryset-redefining apps is not clearly defined by Django, which makes it a bit random. It happens to work pretty well with MPTT because what they change in the queryset happens to be mostly compatible with what hvad does with it (with some edge cases like you just found).

I guess this specific case could deserve a compatibility package, or maybe creating some `hvad.contrib.mptt` submodule, so it can be well defined, well behaved and well tested. There are quite some opportunities for optimization as well, if someone is willing to dig into it.

Oh. I see. Interesting. Insulting your users by insinuating they don't know how Python. I know what exceptions are. I've used Python for a decade. This one is called Value _Error_, and as the name suggests, is meant to signal one thing: errors.

Co-opting Python's most common exception type to mean "unhandled WebSocket packet type" seems ill-conceived, especially considering the user very likely wants to know when packets are being dropped. All in all, a pretty bad way to deal with the problem.

More so, if your user's code errors with that exception type, which is raised by basically every single library both standard and PyPI, your library simply silences it -- because it _might_ mean the last WebSocket packet's type isn't recognized by the library.

I urge you to rethink this design.

> Do you need a brief primer of how to use DRF-Mongonengine? I'm writing a
> post at medium.com about how to use it.
>
> I'm currently using DRF-Mongonengine in combination with Postgres, which
> is used to store users. (Although, if you want to use DRF's authentication,
> you can easily migrate to Postgres).
>
> Basically, you need to set up a connection with Mongo database in
> settings.py, you need to create a DRF-Mongoengine router to serve your
> viewsets and for each view you need to create a triplet
> (document-serialzier-view).
>
> Connection with database can be established in different ways, depending
> on which version of Mongoengine you're using. If it's mongoengine<10
> (probably, 0.8.7 or 0.9), than it relies on pymongo==2, so you can say:
>
>         mongoengine.connect(
>             db=MONGODB_DATABASES['default']['TEST']['name'],
>             host=MONGODB_DATABASES['default']['host']
>         )
>
> Than in urls.py you create a router like this:
>
> from rest_framework_mongoengine.routers import DefaultRouter
> router = DefaultRouter()
> router.register(r'tool', ToolViewSet, r"tool")
>
> And you define your model-serializer-view triplets as follows:
>
> from mongoengine import Document
> from rest_framework_mongoengine.serializers import DocumentSerializer
> from rest_framework_mongoengine.viewsets import ModelViewSet
>
> class Tool(Document):
>     label = mf.StringField(required=True)
>     description = mf.StringField(required=True, null=True)
>
> class ToolSerializer(DocumentSerializer):
>     class Meta:
>         model = Tool
>
> class ToolViewSet(ModelViewSet):
>     lookup_field =  'id'
>     serializer_class = ToolSerializer
>
>     def get_queryset(self):
>         return Tool.objects.all()
>
> Feel free to ask me in gitter
> <https://gitter.im/BurkovBA/django-rest-framework-mongoengine>, if you
> need help.

I would like better constant support for symbols (and consequently, `HybridBlock`s) as well.

A workaround you can use for now though is to do it as follows. In the block that needs them, make your constants parameters; name each constant parameter something like 'constant_x' (this naming makes it impossible for usual initializer patterns to init them), assign the parameter a custom initializer that will set it to the right value and have your constant block wrap them with a block grad operation so that no optimization will ever touch them.

Everything up to (exclusively) ```RUN /work/ubuntu_adduser.sh``` is cached and everything afterwards is not cached. Usually, this is no problem since docker is able to fuse multiple layers together into a temporary one which is not being put into the docker cache. The problem here is that ```RUN /work/ubuntu_nvidia.sh``` creates a new unfusable permanent layer, which results in always creating a new docker cache entry and thus filling up the disk.

When my program starts I crawl the parts of repo that I'm interested in to
build an in-men representation of the fs (and use watchman to keep it
up-to-date).

I had assumed that using watchman's query to generate a list of files based
on a pattern, suffix, and dirs would be faster than going to disk manually.
However, so far I l've found that recursively reading the directory and
filtering in node is faster by 500ms-1s.

I've found that query's performance is highly correlated by how much
filtering is done by watchman. For example adding 'suffix' and dirname
martchers cut the time for the query from 10s to 5s.

I'm a bit sad that this didn't turn out to be a win.

In that case, where you just define it as `bar = Instance(Bar)`, the default value will be `None`. If I changed that to `Undefined` in this PR, it would behave like Enums, with no default value and an error if you try to access it before it's set to something.

If you provide arguments, like `bar = Instance(Bar, (1,))`, it will instantiate a default value with `Bar(1)`. Do you still want that to happen at instantiation, or should it also be delayed until first access?

I think `None` is a bit confusing here, because there's multiple levels where we want to allow something to be unspecified, so we now have three sentinels in traitlets: None, NoDefaultSpecified and Undefined. I wonder if we could get rid of one of those...

I have a workaround for this. 

Install `awscli` as python lib:
```
pip install awscli
```
Then define this function:

```python
import awscli.clidriver

def aws_cli(*cmd):
    old_env = dict(os.environ)
    try:

        # Environment
        env = os.environ.copy()
        env['LC_CTYPE'] = u'en_US.UTF'
        os.environ.update(env)

        # Run awscli in the same process
        exit_code = awscli.clidriver.create_clidriver().main(cmd)

        # Deal with problems
        if exit_code > 0:
            raise RuntimeError('AWS CLI exited with code {}'.format(exit_code))
    finally:
        os.environ.clear()
        os.environ.update(old_env)
```
To execute:
```python
aws_cli('s3', 'sync', '/path/to/source', 's3://bucket/destination', '--delete')
```

That [\_\_init\_\_.py in utils](https://github.com/robotframework/Selenium2Library/blob/master/src/Selenium2Library/utils/__init__.py) looks like we have to find better way to import the modules for testing.

Once support for multiple values is dropped I think whether we should return `Value` or `Value.value` depends on this: Currently for [len](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/master/nixio/section.py#L135) and [del](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/master/nixio/section.py#L149) item we operate just on `props`. For [items](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/master/nixio/section.py#L178) and [contains](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/master/nixio/section.py#L185) we check both `props` and `sections`. For consistency we should either 1) always iterate over just `props`, or 2) always iterate over `props` and `sections`.

So for option 1), I think we should everywhere only iterate on `props` since generally I imagine when you iterate on a section you want to iterate over its properties not also its sections. If you also iterate on the sections you'd need to do a test to check whether what you received is a section or a property with e.g. `isinstance`, so you might as well explicitly iterate of the section's `props` and `sections` separately which would save you a `isinstance`.

So for option 1 I think we should return `Value.value` since that would be the most convenient. And since it iterates only on `props` this should be fine. For option 2, if we iterate over `props` and `sections` I think we should return the `Value` itself, that way when iterating you'd be able to know if you have a `Property` or `Section`.

I prefer option 1 since it's probably what most want by default, I think.

I think the desire behind what lead to iterating over `props` and `sections` currently, might be better served by having an explicit walk method which iterates through the section/property tree in a breadth or depth first manner. Perhaps it could have a depth parameter which when e.g. set to 1 would walk `props` and `sections` like current behavior. Or it could be like `os.walk` that returns the list of properties (filenames) for every section (directory)?

For more things to do, we have not talked about `__setitem__` :bulb:. `__setitem__` accepts a [`S`](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/master/nixio/section.py#L28), a value, or a list of values and creates a section or properties, respectively. Now that we're dropping support for multiple values it think it should act as follows. Instead of, or in addition to accepting `S` (not sure the significance of `S` as it seems to be only internal?) it should also except a dict. This would create a section with name `key` whose properties are the keys/values from the dict. This way we can create a section/property hierarchy easily though dict assignment.

I can work on implementing these things when we agree on how it should be. Also, what's the timeline on dropping support for multiple properties (although I guess we don't have to wait for that)?

Apparently, a setup.cfg's option will override its corresponding `setup()` argument if the value passed evaluates to false:
* `setup.py`:
```python
from setuptools import setup
dist = setup(zip_safe=False, install_requires=[])
print('install_requires', dist.install_requires)
print('zip_safe', dist.zip_safe)
```
* `setup.cfg`:
```cfg
[options]
zip_safe = True
install_requires = foobar
```
* output of `python setup.py --name`:
```
UNKNOWN
install_requires ['foobar']
zip_safe True
```
Which I find pretty horrible...

`numpy.distutils.misc_util.quote_args` (https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/numpy/distutils/misc_util.py) check if there is a quote on the first char, then before `/LIBPATH:path`. while `library_dir_option` check quotes on `path`.

Maybe there is two way to fix the problem:
- monkey-patch `numpy.distutils.misc_util.quote_args` with `a[-1]` instead of `a[0]` if it exists
- or we should patch `gen_lib_options` like numpy instead of `library_dir_option`.
Both should work.

> I am not explicitly using requests, just using whatever the Stripe API uses by default. 

So we have kind of a fallback system so that we use Requests if it's available (we `import requests` and use it unless there was an error). So I guess the real question here is, is Requests available from your runtime? (I think it probably is.)

> Wouldn't it make sense for the Stripe API to close the session after I call it? Instead of having me do it in my code.

So if you take a look at that other thread, this really is a feature and not a bug. Establishing a new connection to Stripe has a few round trips worth of overhead and it's a common convention in HTTP to re-use a connection to avoid this initial cost of construction. By leaving that connection open, Requests is making your subsequent HTTP calls a little faster, which is a rational default.

If anything, this warning should probably be removed, because it's not actually something that will lead to a problem. If it's really bothering you though, you can get rid of it using something like the code above.

Hey @agilgur5, thanks for reporting this.

Top-level resources can be updated without being retrieved first with the `modify` method:

```python
customer = stripe.Customer.modify('cus_...', description="New description")
```

However, the bindings currently don't offer a way of doing this for nested resources (such as card objects nested in customer objects). I'm tagging this as "future" for now.

In the meantime, if you absolutely need a way to update cards directly, you can do this with:

```python
card = stripe.Card.construct_from({'id': 'card_...', 'customer': 'cus_...'}, None)
card.exp_year = 2020
card.save()
```
It is terrible default behaviour indeed.  I know this may not be maximally consistent, but I think it's a move in the right direction.

As discussed IRL, I think this is a serialisation issue only and we should just use the same solution already created in Records-REST, that allows overriding default behaviour with factories.

We're not compatible with S3, and it will take a great deal of work to be, but I don't see that S3 compatibility causes any trouble in this respect. S3 compatibility primarily dictates URL structure. Headers/resource serialisation also needs to be S3 compztibiite, but that can easily be implemented on top of the solution we have in Records REST.

If it at some point becomes a question of S3 compatibility vs custom solution (note: I don't think this will be the case), then I would go for S3 compatibility. Compatibility with an existing widely used protocol is more important, as it opens for usage of existing client libraries etc.

Your code implies building a new docker image for each instance. Even though it is based on a base, that will quickly become chaos for us I think. Running it with pure configs is much quicker in our deployment setup, and way easier to test. Everyone shares the same image, and the customisations from there are configs. That does not mean that we can not put instance specific files in the image and use environment variables to choose which ones to use, but it is not ideal.

And facets is something that is set up differently for each instance, so it would be nice to find a way to configure them easily. We already have something close to your scripting example, so we will stick with that for now.

The ES [range query](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/query-dsl-range-query.html) falls in two types:

- TermRangeQuery (strings)
- NumericRangeQuery (dates + numbers)

Both types needs one or more of the following parameters: ``gt``, ``gte``, ``lt``, ``lte``

The REST API can be queried like this:

- ``/api/records/?test=<value1>&test=<value2>``

With will result in *one* call to the ``inner`` method with ``values`` being a list ``[<value 1>, <value 2>]``

Issues:
- Handling multiple input values -> should produce multiple range queries or perhaps fail.
- Parsing and validating of one input value, and mapping this into one or more of the parameters (``gt``, ``gte``, ``lt``, ``lte``). 

Regarding the parsing we could do something along the lines of:
- ``1940--2009`` -> ``gte=1940, lte=2009``
- ``--2009`` -> ``lte=2009``
- ``1940--`` -> ``gte=1940``
- ``1940`` -> ``gte=1940``
- ``>1940--<2009`` -> ``gt=1940, lt=2009``
- ``--<2009`` -> ``lt=2009``
- ``>1940--`` -> ``gt=1940``
- ``a--m`` -> ``gte=a, lte=m``
- ...

In case the parsing fails, I think we should raise a ``invenio_rest.errors.RESTValidationError``:

```python
raise RESTValidationError(errors=[FieldError('test', 'Invalid range format.')])
```

This will result in a 400 HTTP error, with a message staying that the format is invalid.

When you specify a delimiter, we need to decode the bytes fetched from s3 and replace the existing delimiter in there with the delimiter you want. We decode the bytes assuming 'utf-8' encoding.

From the error message:
```
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe4 in position 8191: unexpected end of data
```
It looks like the encoding of the result is not in 'utf-8'. Did anything changed recently at your end which changed the encoding from utf-8 to something else. Or are these new types of queries which encode result in some other encoding and don't use utf-8?

One workaround I can thing of is to not specify the delimiter while calling `get_results`. And then read the file and replace the delimiter yourself by using the encoding in which the results are.

Hi Boaz,

I am assuming that what you need is the shortest path distance within the paths defined by the tree structure. For a rooted tree, the minimal path distance is defined using the property of the root (so it would not be very efficient to modify it). The fastest way I can think of is to take the path towards the root for a pair of nodes and find the branching point at which they merge. From this you can extract the "shortest path distance".

Please let me know if this makes sense.

Lida

Reopening because I'm curious about something else pertaining to this.

In my web app, I normally have a web-based flow where to connect to Azure AD, my server redirects the user to the /authorize URL on the endpoint, and when v2 endpoint is active, I include the scope= parameter. When processing the callback, I call adal's `acquire_token_with_authorization_code` to turn this into a usable token for Graph API requests. As long as I follow the rules I mentioned above this works with the v2 endpoint.

Now I want to support a slightly different scenario that is a direct server-to-server communication not involving a specific end user account. My goal is to have a "Test Azure connection" button that admins can run to ensure that they have valid settings for the AAD App ID, tenants, and client secret. I want them to be able to run this without signing in to an Azure account since they're verifying a direct server-to-server connection.

Instead of redirecting the web user, the server code just directly calls adal's `acquire_token_with_client_credentials` using the AAD Graph resource ID and this seems to work fine with ADAL in the v1 endpoint.

According to the V2 endpoint docs here under "Get a token":

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/active-directory-v2-protocols-oauth-client-creds

The v2 process is basically the same except I set the `scope` parameter instead of the `resource` one. Unfortunately ADAL.py doesn't seem to allow for a way to do that. My request results in:

AADSTS90014: The required field 'scope' is missing.

I could construct the `/token` URL and run the request myself but I was hoping to keep that kind of logic in ADAL. It would be nice if `acquire_token_with_client_credentials` and related methods had a way to pass in the scope with a keyword argument.

In the global strategy, there are two analysis files: the upstream ("valid") analysis, and the downstream ("invalid") analysis. 

Before compiling anything, pants splits the analysis for sources in newly-invalidated targets out of the valid analysis and merges it into the invalid analysis (which will start out empty in a clean build). This is "prepare-analysis". 

Then pants splits the invalid analysis into one file per compile chunk. This is "partition-analysis".

Then, after each compile chunk succeeds, pants merges that now-valid analysis into the upstream analysis ("update-upstream-analysis") and trims it from the downstream analysis ("trim-downstream-analysis").

Now each operation on an analysis file is linear in the size of that file, naturally. And that's roughly linear in the number of source files, which is roughly linear in the number of targets. So you'd expect each of these to take longer as compilation progresses. In fact, you'd expect the total compilation to be quadratic in the number of targets.

This is why chunking is such a win, even at the expense of a little bit of dependency resolution correctness. It's still technically quadratic, but there are in practice a small, bounded number of chunks.

If you want to compile target-by-target (or in small chunks) then the isolated strategy is a much better solution - it doesn't do any analysis tricks. Instead the analysis for each target is kept in a separate file, and the incremental compiler just has a large number of upstream elements. I think JMake doesn't even do anything with upstream analysis? Not sure about that. Zinc certainly does, but it caches them, so as long as the nailgun stays alive it doesn't have to keep re-parsing. 

Compiling target-by-target has other performance downsides, especially in Scala, but if you're going to do it, the global strategy is definitely not a good fit. 

So my next question would be, why is Pants chunking so small in this build? Why isn't it compiling all those source files in one pass?

Found a solution for that:

Open the urls.py at root of your project

First of all import the correct library to use the Sitemap direct from aldryn newsblog

```
from cms.sitemaps import CMSSitemap
from aldryn_newsblog.sitemaps.sitemap import NewsBlogSitemap
````

After the place where you define the urlpatterns, do:

```
sitemaps = {
    'cmspages': CMSSitemap,
}

for lang, _ in settings.LANGUAGES:
    sitemaps['newsblog-%s' % lang] = NewsBlogSitemap(language=lang)

urlpatterns += patterns('django.contrib.sitemaps.views',
                        url(r'^sitemaps\.xml$', 'sitemap', {'sitemaps': sitemaps}),
                        )
```

I hope that helps everybody!

Hi Eelco,

Strength-of-connection usually varies in [0, 0.5], if you're using the
"classical" measure.  I would probably not use the symmetric measure.  You
could also try the "evolution" measure, which again has a tolerance that
varies between about [0, 0.5]. In both cases, a value of 0.25 or 0.33 is
typical.  There's also the choice of interpolation, which is probably most
important if you use the root_node_solver.  We can discuss this, too, if
you want.

I don't have a good idea about the eigenvalue error.  I see that you're
using (probably) the smoothed aggregation solver.  This should give you an
SPD preconditioner for a standard Laplacian (mathematically provable).
It's possible that LOBPCG needs to be tuned, that something is going on in
the eigensolver.  Hmmm....

- Jacob

https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/3995f70c1cb6190352eb5e063e3ba37b3121112f/training_config/bidaf.json#L31-L35

> I can't agree, @ukoethe, with regard to builds strings.

That's very reasonable as long as the desired functionality is achieved in a different way. But then the build string should be eliminated from version comparison entirely, because the code at  `conda/resolve.py`, lines 388 and 396 is very suspicious and contradicts the documentation (http://conda.pydata.org/docs/spec.html#package-metadata says that the build string is not used). 

I'm also not sure about the future role of the build number (technically a part of the build string). Are higher build numbers simply shadowing lower ones, or is the rule more complex? For example, if build 1 defines a feature, but build 2 doesn't, which one will version resolution consider if the feature is being tracked? If build numbers are to remain a part of the build string and continue to take part in version resolution (which I think will be the case), there should at least be an unambiguous syntax to indetify the build number in the build string, and improved documentation.  

> plugin architecture for version ordering

This is a good idea, although I'm not sure if a full fledged plugin architecture is necessary. Probably it will be sufficient to provide a small and fixed set of schemes to pick from. Plugins always imply the possibility that a users doesn't have the plugin, which worsens the installation experience.

Yes, handling transaction restarts in this way requires intrusive changes to many apps. We've made some changes that reduce the likelihood of transaction restarts, so you *might* be able to get by without surrounding everything in a retry loop, but these restarts remain more common with CockroachDB than in other databases and I think you'll still want retry handling. 

I appreciate the feedback about potential interfaces; I've never used the `Session` portions of sqlalchemy so I don't know what patterns are "natural" with it. Can you explain this `achieve_value_consensus` method a little more to me? Why does it use two transactions?  We might be able to allow the pattern you're describing in the text of your comment, in which a single call to `commit` or `rollback` could be allowed within a `run_transaction` callback (and then any subsequent attempts to use the transaction would fail). However, the fact that you do a separate query after committing the initial transaction wouldn't work in this model - you'd need full nested transaction support to make this work as written. 

My recommendation for now is to think of `run_transaction` (or something like it) as *portable*. It's not the way you're writing your app currently, but once you've made the change, you won't be locked in to cockroachdb. The same pattern will work for any database (and in fact could be desirable even on other databases to handle deadlocks). 

If `achieve_value_consensus` used a single transaction, I would write it like this (note that reading before the write is going to be substantially faster than writing first and catching the IntegrityError in CockroachDB):

```
def achieve_value_consensus(self, key, value):
    log.info('Achieve consensus on key `%s`.', key)
    def callback(dbsession):
        try:
            return dbsession.query(ConfigItem).filter_by(key=key).one().value
        except sqlalchemy.orm.exc.NoResultFound:
            dbsession.add(ConfigItem(key, value))
            return value
    run_transaction(_engine, callback)
```

Since the entire function is now in one transaction, a decorator form of `run_transaction` might be more convenient.

# Installation instruction
## Package azure-mgmt-datamigration
You can install the package `azure-mgmt-datamigration` of this PR using the following command:
	`pip install "git+https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python@restapi_auto_datamigration/resource-manager#egg=azure-mgmt-datamigration&subdirectory=azure-mgmt-datamigration"`

You can build a wheel to distribute for test using the following command:
	`pip wheel --no-deps "git+https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python@restapi_auto_datamigration/resource-manager#egg=azure-mgmt-datamigration&subdirectory=azure-mgmt-datamigration"`

If you have a local clone of this repository, you can also do:

- `git checkout restapi_auto_datamigration/resource-manager`
- `pip install -e ./azure-mgmt-datamigration`


Or build a wheel file to distribute for testing:

Sub-application has a context: state aka `app['name']`, own signals and middlewares. Why do we need another stateful concept?
`RoutesDef` in opposite is just a state-less list of route definitions but with convenient methods like `routes.get(...)` for usage as web-handler decorators.

removing pipelining would simplify request handling flow. (some speedup is expected too) 
pipelining requires read/write ordering. I doubt pipelining is in use, especially for python servers.
+1 to drop. This header is not what to add for the requests. It's the server response header.

I'm kinda wondering if this works (based on your earlier example, not the most recent version):

```python
from autograd import elementwise_grad, jacobian
import autograd.numpy as np

def wrapper(args):
    return sum(a**2 for a in args)

inp = [1000*np.random.rand(10,1), np.random.rand(1,10), np.random.rand(1,10), np.random.rand(1,10), np.random.rand(1,10), np.random.rand(1,10)]
inp = np.broadcast_arrays(*inp)
array_inp = np.concatenate(map(lambda x: np.expand_dims(x, 0), inp))

print(array_inp.shape)
gres = elementwise_grad(wrapper)(array_inp)
print(gres.shape)
hess = jacobian(lambda x: elementwise_grad(wrapper)(x).sum(2).sum(1))(array_inp)
print(hess.shape)
```

It attempts to use the same trick as in `elementwise_grad` to support broadcasting (i.e. summing across dimensions effectively broadcasts the gradient). It has the right shape at least!

Not quite - a potential workflow would be something like this:

 - Use ``test`` and ``lint`` to develop tools locally.
 - Use ``serve`` to review tool. Ready to publish...
 - Use ``shed_init`` to create ``.shed.yml``.
 - Write ``tool_dependencies.xml``.
 - Use ``shed_upload --force_repository_creation --shed_target testtoolshed`` to populate the tool on the test tool shed.
 - Use ``shed_test --target testtoolshed`` to test published tools. This will start a Galaxy in the background, install the tool(s) from the test tool shed and its dependencies, and run the tests with tool shed dependency resolution (instead of local resolution configured with ``test``).
 - Publish to main tool shed.

I don't think I would use ``shed_serve`` in a typical workflow - but if you want to do ``serve`` but with tool shed installed tools instead of local copies - that is what it would do. (More for consistency and as a stepping stone to ```shed_test``.)

The goal with #19 is more to have a ``test`` that works like ``shed_test`` but without having to upload the packages to the tool shed and install and test them. If you had a really robust variant of #19 you can imagine you could eliminate publishing to the test tool shed and running ``shed_test`` from this workflow - you would be confident the thing you upload passes the tests before publishing them.

This snippet is where the file path is converted to URL [lines 28-36](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/886a6ac41c685f20d39e352f9c657e59f3312265/cwltool/stdfsaccess.py#L36):
```
class StdFsAccess(object):
    def __init__(self, basedir):  # type: (Text) -> None
        self.basedir = basedir

    def _abs(self, p):  # type: (Text) -> Text
        return abspath(p, self.basedir)

    def glob(self, pattern):  # type: (Text) -> List[Text]
        return [file_uri(str(self._abs(l))) for l in glob.glob(self._abs(pattern))]
```

`file_uri` is imported from `schema_salad.ref_resolver`

In `ref_resolver.py`, [file_uri](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/schema_salad/blob/master/schema_salad/ref_resolver.py#L53) calls [urllib.request.pathname2url](https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.request.html#urllib.request.pathname2url).

The call to `pathname2url` is causing `@` to be converted to `%40` and spaces converted to `%20`.

The issue is the conversion happens after the CWL workflow is complete and moving files to the output path. 

In the example above, `/tmp/tmpc3_Od4/a b` exists but CWLTool is looking for `/tmp/tmpc3_Od4/a%20b`.

I can modify (line 36)[https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/886a6ac41c685f20d39e352f9c657e59f3312265/cwltool/stdfsaccess.py#L36] in `stdfsaccess.py` from `return [file_uri(str(self._abs(l))) for l in glob.glob(self._abs(pattern))]` to `return [urllib.request.url2pathname(file_uri(str(self._abs(l)))) for l in glob.glob(self._abs(pattern))]` and this workflow will complete successfully. 

I'm not sure that's the best fix. It seems like a fix in `schema_salad` `ref_resolver.py` would be more appropriate.

If proposed segment is going to just run that command it is better to add segment that runs arbitrary shell command. Unless you are going to make that script a library which will only fetch data, but formatting will be done by powerline itself, tmux-mem-cpu-load output is not in line with the rest of the segments at all:

1. `--powerline-right` outputs compiled-in arrow, powerline takes delimiters from the configuration. Also powerline does not expect locale to be only UTF-8.
2. Available memory is expected to be formatted using `powerline.lib.humanize_bytes.humanize_bytes`. This as well means that one can configure whether to show GiB or GB.
3. There is already a segment for load average, so load average should not be displayed by your segment.
4. There are a few graphs (at least, battery), and they are more configurable: at least, you can replace `|` and ` ` (space) with something.
5. There is already a segment for CPU usage, so CPU usage should not be displayed by your segment. If needed, it is what should be adjusted (including possibly using your library should it be a library), as well as adjusting this for bar.
6. It is expected that memory usage segment will use a gradient color.
7. I do not see how this segment may be considered tmux-specific (i.e. it should not be in `powerline.segments.tmux`).

---

In any case, you do not have to maintain a fork, powerline is absolutely fine with taking segment function from any package.

I'm not sure that we should implicitly merge when duplicate keys are used for `dump_to`. I think we should error instead of silently overwriting data.

Have you tried implementing this behavior with a `post_dump` method instead of `dump_to`?

```
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, post_dump

class MyPayload(Schema):
    class ActionAdd(Schema):
        item_name = fields.Str()
    
    class ActionRemove(Schema):
        ref_number = fields.Int()
    
    add_items = fields.Nested(ActionAdd, many=True)
    remove_items = fields.Nested(ActionRemove, many=True)
    
    @post_dump
    def merge_items(self, data):
        data['items'] = data['add_items'] + data['remove_items']
        del data['add_items']
        del data['remove_items']
        return data
```

If the use case ever needs special ordering or conditional logic, this pattern would have to be used anyway.

```
my_mod = dyn_mod_gen()
m1, m2 = pickle_depickle([my_mod, my_mod])

# m1 is identical to m2, but they are different from my_mod
```

Hi @thequilo ,
I know this is confusing, but it is intended behavior: named configs behave like updates, and not like normal configs (ideally I would have called them named_updates instead). 
The usecase for which they are intended is to store a certain set of parameters that make sense together. For example if I report multiple experiments in my paper, I like to have a named config for each, such that running them is easy. But for that they must be able to influence the config values of ingredients, so that you can for example set the dataset-ingredient and the network parameters in one named config. 

Because of the way Sacred evaluates configurations, that means they have to be evaluated before the ingredient config is evaluated. So in your example `ing` does not have a value for `a` yet, when `named` is evaluated.

So what can be done? We could implement an actual named config, i.e. something that behaves like a normal configuration but that is optional. You can simulate that in your code by doing something along these lines:
``` python
@ex.config
def config1(ing):
   named_configs = []
    b = ing['a'] * 3.1415

@ex.config
def named(named_configs, ing):
    if 'named' in named_configs:
        b = ing['a'] * 2
```
and then run with 
```
$ python test.py print_config with "named_configs=['named']"
```

I like the idea of having a feature like this built-in, but to avoid increasing the amount of confusion should probably include renaming the current named_config to named_update or so. I'll put it on my list, but since it would break the API, it won't happen before the next major release, which I'm afraid is not going to happen this year.

Does this clarify things for you? What is your opinion about this?

Fine by me. 

One remark though: the user may expect the `max_depths` to work even if `check_scan_dir` is overriden, which will not be the case here. 

A workaround would be:
```python
class BaseWalker(object):
# ... #
def _check_scan_dir(self, fs, path, info, depth):
    return (self.max_depth is None or depth < self.max_depth) \
       and self.check_scan_dir(fs, path, info, depth)

def check_scan_dir(self, fs, path, info, depth)
    """Override me !
    """
    return True
```

That way, `check_scan_dir` would be easier to override. (This may be applied to the other `check` functions as well).

Hi @chrisranderson,

This looks really cool! I'd love to support such an ambitious and interesting project if it's technically feasible.

Right now, every TensorBoard plugin gets its data via the summary system. Ie, they get data from event files that are written to disk by the summary.FileWriter. It's purely one-way communication, and it has high latency, because TensorBoard ingests data from the event logs at most every 5 seconds. Also, everything written there is by default persisted forever on disk, so if we use it for high-throughput communication it will quickly saturate disk. So, the summary system as presently written is inappropriate for any real-time streaming application.

@jart is working on revamping the summary system to use sqlite and to support data streaming, so I'll let her chime in on whether she thinks the new summary system would be a good fit for your application.

@caisq has worked on establishing direct 2-way grpc communication between TensorFlow and TensorBoard. It would be ideal if we could leverage his work, but we've had difficulty open-sourcing it due to issues with some dependencies.

I think it would also be feasible for you to develop your own system, specific to this plugin, for getting data from TensorFlow to TensorBoard, and setting up 2way communication. Something like the following:

Let's suppose your plugin is called the RealTimeParameterVisualizer (maybe we'd come up with something catchier later :stuck_out_tongue:). Then you create a class `tensorboard.plugins.real_time_parameter.ParameterWriter`. The ParameterWriter is instantiated with a pointer to the logdir. And the user modifies their training code so that every step, it offers the model weights and the gradients to the ParameterWriter. 

On instantiation, the ParameterWriter makes a directory within the logdir like `logdir/plugins/real_time_parameter` which contains a file called `mode`, which will be used by TensorBoard to communicate to the ParameterWriter.

On the TensorBoard backend side, you create `tensorboard.plugins.real_time_parameter.RealTimeParameterPlugin`. The RealTimeParameterPlugin is given the logdir by TensorBoard framework, and it takes responsibility for writing the `mode`. So based on user interaction it can change the mode from "off" to "parameter values", "gradient values", "gradient variance", etc.

The ParameterWriter uses poll checking to see when the `mode` changes from off. When the mode is not off, it begins dumping data to the filesystem containing the compressed parameter data for the frontend to visualize. We can think of an appropriate way to make sure that the amount of disk space used is bounded, e.g. by having the PW write to a new file every minute, and giving it responsibility for deleting (or downsampling) data older than 10 minutes. 

In the example you gave of  the million parameter model, if we want to show 16-value greyscale for each parameter at 30 FPS, that would be (10^6 values * 0.5 bytes/value * 30 per second) = 15MB/s which seems reasonable for writing/reading to disk, and processing without too much latency. Or, if we were willing to have 1 update per second, then it would be just 500KB/sec. 

@jart / @wchargin Please share your thoughts too.

Now to answer your questions:

> 1. Has this been done before in a way that is meant for lots of people to use? I couldn't find anything from a bit of Googling around, just people doing one-off things for their projects.

I'm not aware of any widely accessible version of this. I think it would be novel work, and quite valuable to the community.

> 2. Can this be visualized as the model is running, as fast as every iteration without drastic slowdowns? For instance, using a 1 million parameter model where the parameters are reshaped into a 1000x1000 square.

See discussion above. I think we could accomplish something that feels fast to the user, and is near-real-time. 

> 3. Do you have any suggestions of features that you would find useful that I could add? Is there some other visualization tool that I should be building instead?

This seems like an interesting project to me. It could be made technically simpler by taking away the realtime component, and settling for getting data ~once per minute. But, you could focus more on building UI interaction and visualizations to really dive into the data and find ways to interpret the weights in context. E.g. serializing the weights and activations for k training examples, and looking at how the patterns of activations are different for different examples.

> 4. Should I just build a GUI in Python or a standalone JS app instead of integrating with TensorBoard?

You could build a GUI on your own, and it will be easier for you to develop since you'll have control over everything, and won't be limited by TensorBoard's assumptions. However, convincing people to discover and use a new tool is always an uphill battle. I guess that if this is integrated into mainline TensorBoard, the usage will be several orders of magnitude higher than if you make a purely standalone tool.

Also tagging @colah and @shancarter as they may have thoughts to offer.

Nice catch! I think definately we need to signal this inside the Thread Group.

I was thought a little about possible solution, and its hard to find any good.

The solution that you may have in OpenThread looks as it have a weak spot in case parent assigns the same Child ID for subsequent children.

Let's say SED1 gets Child ID equal to 1, then parent drops the child and some other
ED attaches. Parent then may assign also Child ID equal to 1 to the new child, causing
potential danger. For example if the new child is not a SED or it is but pulls first and gets some Child Update Response or some other "rejected" message.

Some potential solution would be to not ACKed for 802.15.4 Data Requests for not our's SEDs. But there are two things probably rejecting the idea:
 - All 802.15.4 Data Requests have to be secured (then MIC will failed for old SED)
 - Send ACK to valid children before ACK Timeout - which may look impossible to met since we need to do decryption here.

Or SED may use extended source address when polling.

I thought of one additional minor concern that could influence the implementation here. Imagine the following:

1. CentOS 6 user runs certbot-auto < 0.21.0.
2. It upgrades to certbot-auto >= 0.21.0 and runs it with `--le-auto-phase`. `LE_PYTHON` is set to Python 2.6 and the version number environment variable is not set.
3. It is able to immediately rebootstrap instead of waiting for a subsequent run of certbot-auto.

In this case, phase 1 is rerun and unless something else happens, `LE_PYTHON` will still be set to Python 2.6. Assuming #5389 or something like it lands, users will get a message about being unable to check for updates because `DeterminePythonVersion` is respecting `LE_PYTHON`. This isn't the end of the world, but if we can implement what you're describing here to avoid this problem, I think we should.

I have a couple ideas, so let me know if what you were thinking doesn't help with this problem.

Also keep in mind, that scipy sparse matrix is limited to 2D.
If we limit ourselves to treatment planning images where slices are commonly used, then we could use a dictionary where key would be slice number and value a corresponding sparse matrix for a slice.

Saving several sparse matrices into a single file is straight-forward and can be done as described in:
https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.sparse.save_npz.html#scipy.sparse.save_npz

If you plan to use the sparse matrices only for TPS-related data, then I suggest to implement it as a feature of pytrip.

**pymchelper** is assumed to be converter where input comes from Monte-Carlo codes (Fluka , SHIELD-HIT) and can be saved in various formats (including TRiP98 format, .dos).

**pytrip** is assumed to deal with TRiP98 data (i.e. read and write .dos). If the aim of this issue is to have sparse matrices for TRiP98 data mostly, then lets put it there. 

Still if you need to convert to sparse matrices file generated by SH12A, then I'd suggest to convert it to the .dos/.hed and then use pytrip sparse matrix format.

Another question is if you want to add somewhere in the file with sparse matrix information kept in TRiP98 header file, like spacing ?

2. Embed group names in task file

sos status

sos status -t align

would list all tasks with tag `align`.

@grantmcconnaughey @ebpetway Here is the workflow I think we should go with.

1. User attempts to log in
2. The view from the framework calls `admin_get_user` before attempting to authenticate and based on the users status we either call `authenticate` because the user's status is **CONFIRMED** or return a redirect response based on the user's status.

## What's Needed to Make it Work

1. Different methods for each scenario
2. Different views for each user status scenario (UNCONFIRMED | CONFIRMED | ARCHIVED | COMPROMISED | UNKNOWN | RESET_REQUIRED | FORCE_CHANGE_PASSWORD)

> (it's certainly confusingly named!)

Really? And I find its naming very natural - it indeed waits for result within specified period of time. Also, "computation model" of it is very natural - a good look at a timeout as an error, so exception is raised, the same as in sync code would do. Yes, it can be recovered (sometimes), and context manager can be built on top of it, but asyncio exactly exposes the proper API level to do all that.

Vice versa, I find both naming and semantic of Trio's move_on_after() to be not natural - it swallows error, and leaves a user with `if`'s on random variables to check whether it happened. (Somehow, move_on_after() is the first thing re: timeouts I saw in Trio. I grepped the tutorial and see that "move_on_after" isn't even there! Instead, it has TODO for fail_after(). @matthiasplappert 
> The problem is that the LSTM needs to be trained on the entire episode from start to finish since otherwise the hidden state of the LSTM will not be correct. 

There is a way around that, although it's annoying enough that I'm not sure it's worth doing. You need two models that are identical except that one has stateful set to False and includes a fixed timestep size greater than one in the input shape. The other has stateful set to True and an input shape that has both batchsize and timesteps set to one.

You train (using fit) on the first model with some number of timestep-size contiguous chunks from memory that don't cross episode boundaries, then you load the weights into the second model and test using one environment step at a time.

In general Calamari is not designed to predict single characters, instead it is designed to predict a complete sequence of characters (sentence) as a whole.
To predict a single character a simple Classification-Network might be better suited (see e.g. MNIST examples)

If you still want to use Calamari:
There are many parameters that could possible effect the accuracy. You could try to increase the --batch_size e.g. to 128. Moreover another network structure could be usefull (--network).
You can also try to limit the alphabet to test if Calamari is able to learn a smaller charset (e. g. 1000, just for testing)

`customizer` branch contains a completed rework of class customizers.  Customizers now simply require using a decorator on an ordinary class to define new methods.  Late customizers (post class construction) are possible.  One issue resulting in a segfault was identified and verified to be an issue in master.  The branch is ready for a pull request once devel is reviewed.
